name: Pontianak
alias: also Kuntilanak · the woman who died in childbirth
body: She announces herself by scent before sight: frangipani, sweet and too heavy, the smell of a grave dressed in flowers. They say a woman who dies in childbirth, or carrying a child, returns this way. From the front she is beautiful, robed in a white dress with hair falling to the waist; from behind she is ruined, a hollow opened in her back where the spine should hold. The loveliness is the lure, and the ruin is what she truly is.

The cry gives the rule that keeps you alive. When her wail sounds faint and far, she is close; when it rings loud, she has already passed you by. She is drawn to the smell of laundry hung out overnight and to new fathers, those men whose households still carry the warmth of a recent birth. She is said to nest in the banana trees, the slow green dark of the kebun, which is why some still tie a red thread around the trunk and leave it there, untouched, season after season.

What lingers is not the scream but the perfume: the way a particular sweetness can turn a familiar street strange, so that for years afterward you cannot pass a flowering frangipani at night without slowing your step. Children in the older estates are still told not to play under the banana clumps behind the block after dark, and more than one grandmother will tell you, plainly, that the night the flowers smell loudest is the night to bring the washing in.
note: The old ward is a nail or a sharp piece of iron driven into the hollow at the nape of her neck. Do this and, the stories say, she becomes a dutiful and ordinary wife, keeping house and bearing children, until the day the nail is ever pulled free, at which point she remembers what she is and is gone. The name itself is sometimes traced to perempuan mati anak, the woman dead of a child, and across the Strait she is more often called Kuntilanak.

In Singapore the densest sightings cluster on the older kampong land and the fringes of Bukit Brown, where the graves are old and the trees grow close. The tale is told and retold in Malay households, in Indonesian horror films, and in the warnings passed to expectant mothers, who in some families are watched and not left alone in the weeks around a birth. It is folklore that still does practical work: a story that tells the living when to be careful.
